findings.title: Consolidate duplicated Gemini chat methods in ai_service.py
findings.what: _chat_with_gemini (line 433, CC=50) and _chat_with_specific_gemini_model (line 888, CC=52) are ~95% identical — both build context, handle images, call the Gemini API, and track usage. The only difference is model selection (auto vs explicit) and config passing. Refactor into a single parametric method with an optional model_name parameter.
findings.why: These two methods account for ~1000 lines of duplicated code and are the #3 and #4 hotspots in the repo. Any bug fix or feature change (e.g., adding streaming, new model params) must be applied twice. Consolidation halves maintenance burden and reduces total backend complexity significantly.

findings.title: Extract ChatInterface sub-components to reduce CC=51
findings.what: ChatInterface.tsx (409 lines, CC=51, nesting=12) is the #1 hotspot. The SVG detection/sanitization logic is duplicated 3x across code, pre, and diagramSvg rendering paths. Extract: (1) MessageBubble component for per-message rendering (~105 lines), (2) DiagramRenderer for shared SVG detection + DOMPurify sanitization, (3) ImageUploadForm for the image preview/attach UI (~72 lines).
findings.why: Highest complexity component in the codebase. The duplicated SVG detection is a security-sensitive code path (DOMPurify sanitization) — having it in one place makes it easier to audit and update. Extraction would drop CC by ~12-15 points.

findings.title: Remove dead diagram helper functions from mutations.py
findings.what: Five functions are dead or behind disabled code paths: _chat_with_diagram_generation (line 686), requests_diagram (line 718-ish area), contains_pattern_info (line 769), extract_pattern_text (line 788), _parse_pattern_response (line 874). These are remnants from before diagram rendering was re-enabled via the matplotlib service. The call sites at lines 434, 498, 503, 655 appear to be inside conditionally-disabled blocks. Remove ~275 lines of dead code.
findings.why: mutations.py is the #2 hotspot (CC=32 for _parse_pattern_response alone). Removing dead code reduces file size and eliminates confusion about which diagram path is actually active. Recent PR #45 re-enabled diagram rendering through the proper matplotlib service, making these helpers obsolete.

findings.title: Step-by-step guided pattern mode
findings.what: Add a 'guided mode' toggle in the chat interface that breaks a pattern into numbered steps and presents them one at a time with visual aids. Backend: add a new mutation (or extend chatWithAssistantEnhanced) that accepts a pattern and returns structured steps with stitch counts, diagrams per step, and difficulty annotations. Frontend: new GuidedMode component showing current step, progress bar (ui/progress.tsx already exists), 'next step' / 'I need help' buttons, and inline diagram rendering per step.
findings.why: The product's stated goal is 'make complex patterns accessible to beginners.' Difficulty levels exist but beginners still face a wall of text. Step-by-step is the #1 feature that would differentiate this from generic AI chatbots — it transforms a conversation tool into a guided crafting companion. The progress.tsx component is already built but unused.

findings.title: Yarn and materials calculator
findings.what: Add a materials calculator that takes a pattern (or project) and returns estimated yarn quantities by weight/color, hook/needle sizes, and other materials needed. Backend: new GraphQL mutation that sends pattern text to AI with a structured output prompt for materials extraction, returning typed MaterialsEstimate. Frontend: MaterialsCalculator panel in ProjectDetailPage showing itemized materials list with quantities. The DB already stores yarn_weight and hook_size — extend to store full materials list as JSON.
findings.why: Crafters' #1 practical question before starting a project is 'how much yarn do I need?' This is listed in product goals and directly serves the target audience of beginners who can't estimate materials from pattern notation alone. It turns the app from a translation tool into a project planning tool.

findings.title: Server-side pattern search and filtering
findings.what: Pattern library filtering (difficulty, category, tags, search) currently happens entirely client-side from the savedPatterns prop. Add server-side GraphQL queries with filter parameters: difficulty level, category, tag matching, and full-text search. Return paginated results instead of loading all patterns at once.
findings.why: As the pattern library grows, client-side filtering won't scale — loading all patterns with base64 images into memory is expensive. Server-side filtering also enables future community sharing (product goal) where users browse patterns from other users, which requires server-side queries by definition.
